subtopic_detail: First-line structured psychotherapy choice for established borderline personality disorder
stem: You are the community mental health team registrar following up a 24-year-old woman with a 4-year history of borderline personality disorder confirmed on structured assessment 12 months ago. She has had three psychiatric admissions for self-harm and suicidal ideation in the past 2 years. She has been on sertraline 200 mg daily and quetiapine 100 mg at night for over a year with limited benefit. Self-harm by cutting occurs around weekly. She is engaged in care, attends appointments and is keen to try therapy. She is not currently psychotic, severely depressed or in active crisis. She lives independently and has a part-time job.

data_table.Examination: Healed self-harm scars to forearms, no fresh wounds; mental state stable
data_table.Investigations: Routine bloods normal, no current intoxication

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her management?

options.text: Increase sertraline to 250 mg daily and add lithium 250 mg twice daily for emotion dysregulation and consider a long-acting injectable antipsychotic if self-harm persists at the next 3-month review
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is not first-line for BPD; structured psychotherapy is the recommended treatment. Polypharmacy without specific indication should be avoided. (Source: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

options.text: Refer for an evidence-based structured psychotherapy program (dialectical behaviour therapy or mentalisation-based therapy or schema-focused therapy) with a target of 12 months of treatment and review pharmacotherapy aiming to rationalise prescribing
options.rationale: NHMRC and RANZCP recommend evidence-based structured psychotherapies (DBT, MBT, SFT, transference-focused psychotherapy) as first-line for BPD with active rationalisation of pharmacotherapy. (Source: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

options.text: Plan a hospital admission for behavioural containment of self-harm with a 4 to 6 week structured ward program, ECT consideration and graded leave because community management has failed over the last 12 months
options.rationale: Long-stay psychiatric admission for BPD is generally counterproductive and not recommended; brief crisis admission only for acute risk. ECT is not indicated. (Source: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

options.text: Discharge to GP-led management with a written safety plan and crisis line numbers, on the basis that BPD generally responds best to non-specialist supportive care with minimal medical intervention
options.rationale: Recurrent self-harm and previous admissions indicate need for specialist structured psychotherapy, not discharge. (Source: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

options.text: Trial open-label naltrexone 50 mg daily and topiramate 100 mg twice daily for impulsivity and self-harm and reassess at 12 weeks before considering psychotherapy referral if symptoms persist on combination pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Naltrexone and topiramate are not recommended as first-line for BPD; psychotherapy is first-line. (Source: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

explanation.summary: BPD is best managed by evidence-based structured psychotherapy programs delivered over at least 12 months: dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT, Linehan), mentalisation-based therapy (MBT, Bateman & Fonagy), schema-focused therapy (SFT, Young) or transference-focused psychotherapy (TFP, Kernberg). NHMRC guidelines recommend these as first-line over pharmacotherapy. Medications are used for specific comorbid disorders (depression, anxiety) or symptom targets (impulsivity), not for BPD as a whole; polypharmacy should be rationalised. Long-stay admissions are generally avoided; brief crisis admission only for acute risk that cannot be safely managed in community. Crisis plans and safety plans are core community components.
key_points: First-line: structured psychotherapy (DBT, MBT, SFT, TFP) 12+ months
Pharmacotherapy: comorbidity-targeted only; rationalise polypharmacy
Long-stay admission avoided; brief crisis admission for acute risk only
Safety plans and crisis lines as part of community care
Family/carer support is recommended
explanation.pearls: Stress prescribing in BPD (adding more drugs after crises) tends to accumulate sedating polypharmacy that worsens function and falls risk without changing self-harm rates; structured deprescribing in stable phases is as important as introducing therapy.

sources.label: NHMRC clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

subtopic_detail: Anorexia nervosa long-term hypogonadism and bone mineral density management
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 22-year-old woman with anorexia nervosa restrictive subtype diagnosed at age 15. She has had two prior medical admissions for bradycardia and electrolyte derangement; her last admission was 14 months ago. She is now stable at BMI 17.2 and engaged with a community eating disorder specialist team for 18 months. Menses have been absent for 5 years; she has never tried to conceive. Recent bloods show normal electrolytes, oestradiol low, LH and FSH low. DEXA scan from 3 months ago shows lumbar spine T-score -2.4 and femoral neck T-score -1.9.

data_table.Examination: No bradycardia symptoms, no lanugo, mild peripheral cyanosis, no fractures
data_table.Investigations: Oestradiol low, LH 1.2, FSH 1.8 (both low), TSH 1.8, prolactin 360 mIU/L, vitamin D 42 nmol/L, corrected calcium 2.30, PTH 4.2 pmol/L, urine pregnancy negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of her bone health?

options.text: Commence a combined oral contraceptive pill 30 microgram ethinyloestradiol/levonorgestrel as the primary treatment for low bone mineral density to provide systemic oestrogen replacement and improve T-scores over 12 months of therapy
options.rationale: COCP has not been shown to improve BMD in AN-related hypogonadism and may give false reassurance of normalised menstruation; transdermal physiological oestrogen replacement has better evidence in adolescents. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Eating disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Eating disorders

options.text: Commence alendronate 70 mg weekly as a first-line bisphosphonate for low T-score in a young woman with hypothalamic amenorrhoea regardless of nutritional restoration progress or her stated plans for future pregnancy in coming years
options.rationale: Bisphosphonates are not first-line in young women with AN due to long bone half-life and uncertainty around fetal exposure if she later conceives; weight restoration with adjunct physiological oestrogen replacement is preferred. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Eating disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Eating disorders

options.text: Refer to endocrinology for consideration of teriparatide 20 micrograms subcutaneously daily for 2 years as a bone-anabolic agent given her premenopausal age and persistent low BMD despite treatment of her primary eating disorder
options.rationale: Teriparatide is reserved for severe established osteoporosis with fragility fracture and is not first-line in young women with AN-related low BMD. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Eating disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Eating disorders

options.text: Continue to support weight restoration as the primary intervention, optimise vitamin D and calcium, consider physiological oestrogen replacement via transdermal oestradiol with cyclical progesterone if BMD does not improve or fractures occur, and repeat DEXA in 12 months
options.rationale: Weight restoration is the most effective intervention for BMD in AN; nutritional sufficiency, vitamin D and calcium optimisation, and physiological transdermal oestrogen replacement when BMD is persistently low or fractures occur are recommended adjuncts. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Eating disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Eating disorders

options.text: Commence denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously every 6 months as an antiresorptive agent because it has rapid onset compared with bisphosphonates and is not deposited in bone matrix to give long-term fetal risk
options.rationale: Denosumab is not first-line in premenopausal AN and has issues with rebound vertebral fracture risk if discontinued; not the recommended choice. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Eating disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Eating disorders

explanation.summary: AN causes hypothalamic amenorrhoea with low oestradiol, low gonadotropins and low IGF-1, plus relative hypercortisolaemia, all of which impair bone turnover. The most effective intervention for BMD is weight restoration; this should always be the primary target. Adjunct measures include optimisation of vitamin D and calcium, treatment of comorbid hypothyroidism or hyperprolactinaemia. Physiological transdermal oestradiol with cyclical progesterone has evidence for improving BMD in adolescents/young adults with persistent amenorrhoea despite weight restoration. COCP does not improve BMD. Bisphosphonates and denosumab are not first-line in premenopausal women with AN due to long-term safety in pregnancy and rebound concerns respectively. DEXA is repeated 1-2 yearly.
key_points: Primary intervention: weight restoration (highest impact on BMD)
Adjuncts: vitamin D, calcium, treat comorbid endocrinopathy
Persistent low BMD: transdermal oestradiol + cyclical progesterone (not COCP)
Avoid bisphosphonates and denosumab as first-line in premenopausal AN
DEXA every 12-24 months while underweight
explanation.pearls: Adolescents with AN can lose peak bone mass at the very age that determines lifetime fracture risk; aggressive weight restoration in the early years is the only intervention with long-term BMD benefit and the brief window before age 25 is when the gains are made.

sources.label: ANZAED clinical practice guideline for eating disorders

subtopic_detail: Distinguishing conversion (functional neurological) disorder from factitious disorder and malingering
stem: You are the neurology registrar reviewing a 32-year-old woman in clinic with a 6-month history of intermittent left-sided weakness, episodes of unresponsiveness with eye closure and convulsive activity. MRI brain, EEG (including ictal recordings of 4 typical events), and CSF are all normal. She is not seeking financial compensation and there is no medico-legal context. On examination, the left-sided weakness shows Hoover's sign and give-way pattern; her grip strength is variable when distracted. Her symptoms began after the death of her father from a stroke. She is distressed by the symptoms and wishes to return to work as a nurse. She does not knowingly produce symptoms and is not seeking medical attention as the primary motivator.

data_table.Examination: Hoover's sign positive on the left, give-way weakness, variable grip when distracted, normal cranial nerves, sensory exam non-anatomical distribution
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain, EEG (4 ictal recordings), CSF all normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Malingering, because the symptoms are inconsistent with any organic neurological process and the variability of weakness on examination indicates intentional production of symptoms for personal gain
options.rationale: Malingering requires intentional symptom production for external incentive (financial, legal, drug-seeking); there is no such incentive here. (Source: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance)
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance

options.text: Factitious disorder imposed on self because the production of seizure-like events without any organic correlate on EEG suggests deliberate symptom feigning to assume the sick role rather than for any external incentive
options.rationale: Factitious disorder requires conscious symptom production with the internal motivation of assuming the sick role; this patient is distressed and wants to return to work, which is incongruous. (Source: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance)
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance

options.text: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) because the symptoms are not consciously produced, are inconsistent with neurological examination, have positive rule-in signs (Hoover's, variable weakness) and are associated with psychological precipitants
options.rationale: FND is diagnosed by positive rule-in signs (Hoover's, distractibility, video-EEG of typical event with normal EEG) without conscious symptom production; the patient is genuinely distressed. (Source: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance)
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder with somatic symptoms because chronic worry can manifest as motor and sensory symptoms in the absence of an underlying organic neurological cause and CBT for anxiety would be first-line
options.rationale: Anxiety with somatic symptoms does not produce non-anatomical motor weakness with positive Hoover sign or non-epileptic seizures; FND is a separate, more specific diagnosis. (Source: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance)
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance

options.text: Multiple sclerosis with normal initial MRI, because MS can present with motor symptoms and atypical seizure-like episodes for years before brain lesions become radiologically apparent on standard imaging sequences
options.rationale: MS does not produce non-anatomical weakness with Hoover's sign or non-epileptic seizures with normal video-EEG; the rule-in signs identify FND positively. (Source: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance)
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder (DSM-5 conversion disorder) is a positive diagnosis made on demonstrating internal inconsistency or incongruence with recognised neurological disease using rule-in signs (Hoover's sign, hip abductor sign, tremor entrainment, video-EEG of typical seizure with normal ictal EEG). It is not a diagnosis of exclusion; relabelling "medically unexplained" symptoms as FND requires positive clinical signs. Distinguishing features from factitious disorder (conscious symptom production to assume sick role) and malingering (conscious production for external incentive) rest on intentionality and motivation. Treatment is multidisciplinary: physiotherapy, psychology (CBT or psychodynamic), patient education about the diagnosis and self-management strategies. Prognosis improves with early diagnosis and engaged treatment.
key_points: FND: positive rule-in signs (Hoover's, distractibility, normal ictal EEG)
Factitious: conscious symptom production for internal sick-role gain
Malingering: conscious production for external incentive (money, drugs, legal)
FND treatment: physiotherapy + psychology + patient education
Early diagnosis and engagement improve prognosis
explanation.pearls: Hoover's sign (involuntary extension of the affected leg when the contralateral leg is flexed against resistance) has very high specificity for functional weakness; demonstrate it to the patient and show that the muscle works, which is the start of treatment, not just diagnosis.

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic symptom and related disorders practice guidance
Stone J. Functional neurological disorders. Practical Neurology 2016

subtopic_detail: Sodium valproate pregnancy prevention program and switch in woman of reproductive age
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 27-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder maintained on sodium valproate 1500 mg daily for 4 years. She is euthymic, currently studying full-time, in a stable relationship and not actively planning pregnancy but does not use any reliable contraception. She does not want to use combined hormonal contraception due to a family history of VTE. She has tried lithium previously but stopped due to fine tremor and weight gain, and tried quetiapine briefly with sedation. She asks for your advice about staying on valproate.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable, no tremor, no acne, no hirsutism
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 540 micromol/L (therapeutic), FBC, LFTs, UEC normal, urine pregnancy negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Continue sodium valproate at current dose, advise her of teratogenic risks and document discussion in the medical record, and recommend she contacts you within 24 hours of any positive pregnancy test result for review
options.rationale: Continuing valproate without reliable contraception in a woman of reproductive age does not meet the TGA pregnancy prevention program requirements; alternative mood stabilisers or contraception planning is required. (Source: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program)
source_refs: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program

options.text: Switch to carbamazepine 200 mg twice daily over 4 weeks as a safer mood stabiliser option in pregnancy planning with lower teratogenic risk than sodium valproate and continue with regular review of mood symptoms during the transition period from valproate to carbamazepine over the next month
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is also teratogenic (neural tube defects, craniofacial); it is not a safer alternative for pregnancy planning. (Source: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program)
source_refs: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program

options.text: Discontinue valproate today and start lamotrigine 25 mg daily with rapid titration to 200 mg daily over 2 weeks to avoid bipolar relapse during transition and minimise the window of unprotected reproductive exposure to teratogenic valproate during the changeover period
options.rationale: Lamotrigine must be titrated slowly over at least 6 weeks to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome; rapid titration is unsafe and abrupt valproate cessation risks relapse. (Source: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program)
source_refs: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program

options.text: Continue valproate at her current dose but enrol her in the formal pregnancy prevention program with monthly pregnancy testing and document a risk discussion review annually; if she fails to comply with the program transition to a non-teratogenic alternative
options.rationale: Pregnancy prevention program requires highly effective contraception; this patient does not use any reliable contraception, so enrolment alone is insufficient. (Source: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program)
source_refs: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program

options.text: Counsel on teratogenic risks of valproate (around 10% major malformation, 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental delay), arrange psychiatric review to plan transition to lamotrigine or olanzapine, and recommend a long-acting reversible contraceptive such as an IUD in the interim
options.rationale: TGA pregnancy prevention program requires risk discussion, transition to non-teratogenic alternative (lamotrigine, olanzapine etc) and effective contraception. LARC (copper IUD or LNG-IUS) is appropriate given her wish to avoid CHC. (Source: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program)
source_refs: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate is associated with major congenital malformation in around 10% of in-utero exposed pregnancies and neurodevelopmental delay in 30 to 40%. The TGA pregnancy prevention program (since 2023) requires that valproate is not used in women of reproductive age unless other treatments are ineffective or not tolerated, the woman is using highly effective contraception, and risks are discussed annually using a risk acknowledgement form. For bipolar disorder in women of reproductive age, lamotrigine (depression-predominant), olanzapine or quetiapine are typical alternatives. Lithium is also teratogenic (Ebstein anomaly, ~0.1% absolute risk) but has lower overall teratogenicity than valproate. Lamotrigine titration is slow (25 mg, 50 mg, 100 mg, 200 mg over weeks).
key_points: Valproate teratogenic risk: 10% major malformation, 30-40% neurodevelopmental delay
TGA PPP: only use if other Rx ineffective AND effective contraception AND annual risk form
Alternatives in reproductive-age women: lamotrigine, olanzapine, quetiapine, lithium
Lamotrigine titration: slow over 6+ weeks to avoid SJS
LARC options if avoiding CHC: copper IUD, LNG-IUS, depot implant
explanation.pearls: The risk acknowledgement form must be signed annually and at each new prescription, not just at initiation; failure to document at each renewal is the most common compliance failure flagged in TGA audits of valproate prescribing.

sources.label: TGA: Sodium valproate (Epilim) pregnancy prevention program

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective disorder versus bipolar I with psychotic features diagnostic discrimination
stem: You are the inpatient psychiatry registrar reviewing a 32-year-old man with a 6-year illness history with recurrent psychotic episodes. Records confirm: 2017 first episode lasting 7 weeks of grandiose delusions and auditory hallucinations of God speaking to him, accompanied by elevated mood, decreased sleep, pressured speech and overspending; 2019 a 6-week depressive episode with low mood, anhedonia and suicidal ideation, during which auditory hallucinations persisted; 2022 a 4-week period of persistent persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations with no concurrent mood symptoms (no mania, no depression). Between episodes he has had mild residual negative symptoms but no severe mood disturbance.

data_table.Examination: Mental state on admission: psychotic with ideas of reference, currently low mood and reduced sleep over 10 days
data_table.Investigations: Bloods routine; no substance use on urine screen; no organic cause identified

lead_in: Which diagnosis is most consistent with his longitudinal illness pattern?

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type, because his illness includes mood episodes (mania, depression) with concurrent psychosis and a 4-week period of psychosis in the absence of mood symptoms during the lifetime of the illness
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires (i) concurrent mood episode with psychosis at some time, (ii) at least 2 weeks of psychosis in the absence of major mood episode during lifetime illness, (iii) mood episodes are present majority of total illness time; his pattern fits. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features, because the psychosis is part of his mood episodes (mania and depression) and the periods of inter-episode psychosis are unrelated negative symptoms rather than schizoaffective psychosis
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features requires psychosis only during mood episodes; the documented 4 weeks of pure psychosis without mood symptoms excludes pure bipolar with psychotic features. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders

options.text: Schizophrenia with comorbid mood episodes, because his persistent residual negative symptoms between episodes are more consistent with schizophrenia and the mood episodes are reactive demoralisation rather than primary affective illness
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires mood symptoms to be present only briefly during total illness; this patient has substantial mood episodes (mania and depression) constituting a major part of his course. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders

options.text: Major depressive disorder with mood-incongruent psychotic features, because his current presentation includes low mood and the persistent delusions and hallucinations represent treatment-resistant psychotic depression following inadequate antidepressant therapy
options.rationale: MDD with psychotic features does not account for the prior manic episode in 2017; the diagnosis must encompass the entire illness course. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic and mood disorder because chronic substance use produces both psychotic and mood phenomena and the patient's current presentation likely represents withdrawal-related affective and psychotic symptoms
options.rationale: No substance use is documented on urine screen and history is not consistent; the diagnosis must be primary. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder DSM-5 criteria require: (A) uninterrupted illness with major mood episode (depressive or manic) concurrent with active phase psychotic symptoms, (B) delusions or hallucinations for 2 or more weeks in the absence of major mood episode during the lifetime of the illness, (C) mood episodes present for majority of total active and residual illness, and (D) not attributable to substance or medical condition. Schizoaffective bipolar type includes manic episodes; depressive type only depressive. Treatment combines antipsychotic plus mood stabiliser or antidepressant as appropriate. Distinguishing from bipolar with psychotic features hinges on criterion B (mood-independent psychosis), and from schizophrenia hinges on criterion C (substantial mood component).
key_points: Schizoaffective DSM-5: mood + psychosis concurrent, plus 2+ weeks pure psychosis lifetime
Mood episodes must be majority of total illness time (active + residual)
Type: bipolar type (mania) vs depressive type
Bipolar I with psychosis: psychosis only during mood episodes
Schizophrenia: mood symptoms brief over total course
explanation.pearls: Documenting the exact temporal relationship between mood and psychotic symptoms over years is essential; without longitudinal records, distinguishing schizoaffective from bipolar with psychotic features is often impossible in cross-section.

sources.label: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and related disorders
